title: Hydrogen-induced volume expansion in hexagonal close-packed iron: Effects of pressure and temperature
authors: Yuichiro Mori, Masahiro Takano, Hiroyuki Kagi, Katsutoshi Aoki, Sho Kakizawa, Noriyoshi Tsujino, Yuji Higo
abstract: Hydrogen is a promising candidate for the light element in terrestrial planetary cores. Its incorporation into iron causes significant volume expansion, leading to a substantial density deficit. Although extensive studies have been conducted on iron hydride (FeH$_{x}$) with the fcc structure, the thermoelastic properties on FeHx with hcp structure (hcp-FeH$_{x}$) remain unconstrained because of the experimental difficulties to control hydrogen content. Here, we synthesized hcp-FeH$_{x}$ with controlled hydrogen contents under high-pressure and high-temperature conditions. We carried out \textit{in situ} X-ray diffraction measurements on hcp-FeH$_{x}$ at 10--25~GPa and 300--900~K using a Kawai-type mutilanvil apparatus and constructed their equations of state. By combining our results with previously reported equations of state for hcp-Fe and experimental determinations of hydrogen content in hcp-FeH$_{x}$, we demonstrated that the discrepancies in the hydrogen-induced volume expansion coefficient can be clearly explained by its pressure and temperature dependence. Our results revealed that the hydrogen-induced volume expansion of hcp-Fe exhibits a strong temperature dependence at low pressures, but its temperature effect significantly weakens with increasing pressure. We also showed that the density reduction of Fe by hydrogenation depends on its crystal structure. These findings demonstrate that estimates of hydrogen content in iron at planetary interior conditions based on hydrogen-induced volume expansion need to be revised by properly accounting for its $PT$-dependence and crystal structure.
summary: This paper investigates how hydrogen causes iron to expand under the extreme pressures and temperatures found in planetary interiors. By measuring hcp-iron hydride, the researchers found that the expansion effect is highly dependent on pressure and temperature, which is crucial for accurately estimating the chemical composition of planets like Mars and Mercury.
main_problem: Determining the pressure and temperature dependence of hydrogen-induced volume expansion in hcp-iron to improve estimates of hydrogen content in planetary cores.
main_result: The study reveals that hydrogen-induced volume expansion decreases with increasing pressure and that its temperature dependence weakens under high pressure. It also shows that density reduction via hydrogenation is larger in the hcp phase than in the fcc phase.
method: In situ X-ray diffraction measurements were performed using a Kawai-type multi-anvil apparatus and a diamond anvil cell under high-pressure (10-25 GPa) and high-temperature (300-900 K) conditions, using ammonia borane as a hydrogen source.
model_or_system: The physical system is hexagonal close-packed iron hydride (hcp-FeHx). The study utilizes the Birch-Murnaghan and Vinet Equations of State (EoS) and the Mie-Grüneisen-Debye model to analyze the PVT relations.
key_observables: Unit-cell volumes, lattice constants (a and c), axial ratio (c/a), hydrogen content (x), and the hydrogen-induced volume expansion coefficient (vH).
important_parameters: Pressures of 10-25 GPa, temperatures of 300-900 K, and hydrogen content x approximately 0.32.
main_assumptions: The hydrogen content is assumed to remain constant during compression and thermal cycles; the use of NaCl as a hydrogen-sealing capsule; and the reliance on specific hcp-Fe EoS models for calculating expansion.
figures_summary: Figures include time-resolved XRD profiles, P-vH curves showing pressure-induced shrinkage, temperature dependence of lattice constants, and plots of hydrogen content stability across different P-T paths.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of planetary core composition, describes the experimental setup and hydrogenation process, presents the derived PVT relations and expansion coefficients, compares hcp and fcc phases, and discusses the geophysical implications for Mercury and Mars.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
